--- title: "Ancient woodland exploration" author: "Ewan McHenry" format: html: theme: cosmo reference-location: margin anchor-sections: true embed-resources: TRUE toc: true code-fold: true default_chunk_options: message: false lightbox: true knitr: opts_chunk: out.width: "100%" fig.height: 7 # Set your desired height editor: visual date: today date-format: "D MMM, YYYY" prefer-html: true execute: echo: false warning: false cache: false cache_refresh: true message: false freeze: auto --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: This is a living document. Any feedback welcomed, particularly if you want to suggest additions or report bugs etc (ewanmchenry\@woodlandtrust.org.uk). # The AWI The AWI used here is an amalgamation of the AWIs from each of the four countries. Notably, these use different methods of categorizing ancient woodland. I have attempted to unify these, based on my understanding of how the different categorizations. It gets a bit crazy[^1], hopefully this figure can help, or at least indicate the complexity of these datasets that some might initially think to be quite simple in their structure. [^1]: I have a few more docs and notes on this, if you're interested, but here is not the place for that detail. ![How are the different AWI types categorised in the different countries' AWIs? How do I unify them to support comparison across coutnries.](images/AWI%20categories%20Sketch_2023-09-01%2015.23.31.excalidraw.sync-conflict-20240810-114956-OXL5OHY.svg) # Area of Ancient Woodland Inventory (AWI) in Different Countries ::: panel-tabset ## Areas of AWI ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## AWI Percentage of Land Cover ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: # Mapping ancient woodland In Scotland, there is more LEPO in the east, and more ASNW There are two hotspots for PAWS ::: panel-tabset ## All AWI categories ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/AW_hexmap-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## ASNW Ancient Semi-Natural Woodland (ASNW) is a subset of the AWI. It is defined as woodland that has been in existence since at least 1600 AD and has been influenced by human activities. ASNW is considered to be of high ecological value and is a priority habitat for conservation. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_asnw-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## PAWS Plantation on Ancient Woodland Sites (PAWS) is a subset of the AWI. It is defined as an ancient woodland site, existing since 1600 AD, on which plantation woodland has been established. PAWS is a priority habitat for restoration. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_paws-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## RAWS Restored on Ancient Woodland Sites (RAWS) is a subset of the AWI. This woodland was formerly PAWS, but now has a canopy cover of at least 50% native species. It is important to note that RAWS is not a subset of ASNW, and that the process of restoration is ongoing on these sites: continued conservation management is needed for them to fulfill their ecological potential. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_raws-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Unknown Ancient Woodland Ancient Woodland Inventory (AWI) data is not perfect, and the type of woodland on some AWI sites is uncertain. This map shows the proportion of hexagons covered by ancient woodland of unknown type. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_unknown-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## SN LEPO Long Established Plantation Origin woodland (Semi-natural). ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_snlepo-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Other LEPO Long Established Plantation Origin woodland (Non semi-natural). ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_otherlepo-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Unknown LEPO AWI data is not perfect, the type of woodland on some LEPO sites is uncertain. This map shows the proportion of hexagons covered by LEPO of unknown type. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_unknownlepo-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Roy SN Roy is a sub-category of ancient woodland used in Scotland with evidence of existence in 1750, but with a potential of a breif unwooded interlude. Roy ancient woodlands appear on the 1750s Roy maps (providing historic evidence of woodland on sites extending beyond the maps on which other types of AWI are largely based), but may not appear as "woodland" on the c. 1850 1st edition OS. Many of these sites do however display many trees on the 1850s maps; the threshold of defining "woodland" on these is somewhat arbitrary. This map shows the proportion of hexagons covered by Roy ancient woodland with semi-natural characteristics. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_roysn-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Roy Other Roy is a sub-category of ancient woodland used in Scotland with evidence of existence in 1750, but with a potential of a brief unwooded interlude. Roy ancient woodlands appear on the 1750s Roy maps (providing historic evidence of woodland on sites extending beyond the maps on which other types of AWI are largely based), but may not appear as "woodland" on the c. 1850 1st edition OS. Many of these sites do however display many trees on the 1850s maps; the threshold of defining "woodland" on these is somewhat arbitrary. This map shows the proportion of hexagons covered by Roy ancient woodland with non semi-natural characteristics. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/hexmap_royother-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: # Designated Ancient Woodland ## SSSI or SAC ::: {.cell} ::: ### Area of SSSI or SAC Ancient Woodland ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Percentage of AWI that is SSSI or SAC ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## SSSI ::: {.cell} ::: *Note that, in N. Ireland, ASSI is the corresponding designation to SSSI.* ### Area of SSSI Ancient Woodland ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Percentage of AWI that is SSSI ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## SAC ::: {.cell} ::: ### Area of SAC Ancient Woodland ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Percentage of AWI that is SAC ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Size of ancient woodland sites An ask came from Nick Phillips about the size of AWI sites, primarily to inform policy around PAWS restoration by acting as evidence RE that commercial viability of e.g. harvest operations at these sites, suspecting that many were small, unviable for commercial forestry, and thus commercial forestery concerns may be less relevant for policy. Here I sought to investigate that speculation. An additional ask was made around the proportion on the public In particular we were interested in the percentage of sites that were \< 3ha, which would essentially make them economically unviable for forestry operations. Below are tables that summarise the site-area distributions of different AWI categories (high and detailed level), with info split by country. First, this is presented for sites (i.e. the number of sites distributed by their area) and then by (i.e. the combined area of sites of different areas). Obviously, while most sites may be small, more area can be within larger sites. Notably, this is not related to habitat patch size, and should not be interpreted ecologically. Often AWI sites are contiguous with others, or other woodland that would effectively form part of the same habitat patch (while still being separate management units). ::: {.cell} ::: ::::::::::::::: panel-tabset ## High Level Categories ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::::::: panel-tabset ### All UK ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: Unknown AWI types are typically much smaller, and are nearly always from Wales. #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_area-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### England ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_england-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: In England, Semi-natural AWI sites are typically smaller than "Other" AWI sites (PAWS). #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_area_england-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### N. Ireland ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_ni-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: In N. Ireland unknown sites (of which there are few) are generally larger than the other categories. #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_area_ni-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### Scotland ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_scotland-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_area_scotland-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### Wales ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_wales-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_high_level_area_wales-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: :::::::: ## Detailed Categories ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::::::: panel-tabset ### All UK ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_area-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### England ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_england-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_area_england-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### N. Ireland ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_ni-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_area_ni-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### Scotland ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_scotland-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_area_scotland-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ### Wales ::: panel-tabset #### By number of sites ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_wales-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: #### By cumulative area ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/fig_awi_site_size_nucat_area_wales-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: :::::::: ::::::::::::::: ## Where are the bigger AW sites? ::: {.cell} ::: ::: panel-tabset ## Sites > 3 ha ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Sites > 10 ha ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Sites > 50 ha ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Sites > 100 ha ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](AW-extent-exploration_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: :::
